#!/bin/bash

# Define an array of audit tools to monitor
AUDIT_TOOLS="/usr/bin/auditctl /usr/bin/aureport /usr/bin/ausearch /usr/bin/autrace /usr/sbin/auditd"

# Create a directory for AIDE configuration if it does not exist
mkdir -p /etc/aide

# Define the AIDE configuration file path
CONFIG_FILE="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

# Check if the AIDE configuration file exists, if not create it with default settings
if [ ! -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Configuration file does not exist, creating a new one."
    cat <<EOF > "$CONFIG_FILE"
root:root:/var/lib/aide/aide.db:/etc/aide/aide.conf:/bin/sh
log_file /var/log/aide.log
check_rootfs yes
use_hash 5
allow_oldconfig no
max_diff_size 1000000
diff_cmd diff -u
EOF
fi

# Add the audit tools to the AIDE configuration file
for TOOL in $AUDIT_TOOLS; do
    if [ ! -e "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        echo "Audit tool $TOOL is not tracked by AIDE."
    else
        grep -q "^$TOOL:" "$CONFIG_FILE" || echo "file $TOOL /etc/aide/audit-tools/$TOOL" >> "$CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
done

# Update the AIDE database and start the AIDE service if it is not running
aide --init-db
systemctl restart aide.service

#!/bin/bash

# Define an array of audit tools to monitor
AUDIT_TOOLS="/usr/bin/auditctl /usr/bin/aureport /usr/bin/ausearch /usr/bin/autrace /usr/sbin/auditd"

# Create a directory for AIDE configuration if it does not exist
mkdir -p /etc/aide

# Define the AIDE configuration file path
CONFIG_FILE="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

# Check if the AIDE configuration file exists, if not create it with default settings
if [ ! -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Configuration file does not exist, creating a new one."
    cat <<EOF > "$CONFIG_FILE"
root:root:/var/lib/aide/aide.db:/etc/aide/aide.conf:/bin/sh
log_file /var/log/aide.log
check_rootfs yes
use_hash 5
allow_oldconfig no
max_diff_size 1000000
diff_cmd diff -u
EOF
fi

# Add the audit tools to the AIDE configuration file
for TOOL in $AUDIT_TOOLS; do
    if [ ! -e "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        echo "Audit tool $TOOL is not tracked by AIDE."

fi
modprobe fips
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Define the FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers in order of strength
fips_ciphers="chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com,aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com,aes256-cbc,aes192-cbc,aes128-cbc,arcfour16,arcfour"

# Check if the SSH server configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    echo "SSH server configuration file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read the current cipher settings from the SSH server configuration
current_ciphers=$(grep '^Ciphers' /etc/ssh/sshd_config | awk '{print $2}' | tr ',' ' ')

# Check if the current ciphers are already set to FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers
if [[ "$current_ciphers" == *"$fips_ciphers"* ]]; then
    echo "SSH server is already configured with FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers."
    exit 0
fi

# Backup the original SSH configuration file
cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak

# Remove the existing Ciphers line and add the FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers
sed -i '/^Ciphers/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo "Ciphers $fips_ciphers" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Restart the SSH service to apply the changes
systemctl restart sshd

echo "SSH server has been configured with FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers."

#!/bin/bash

# Check if automatic graphical login is enabled
AUTOMATIC_LOGIN=$(gsettings get org.gnome.desktop.session idle-delay)

# If automatic graphical login is enabled, disable it
if [ "$AUTOMATIC_LOGIN" -ne 0 ]; then
    gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-delay 0
fi

# Set up a systemd service to ensure the setting persists across reboots
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/systemd/system/disable-automatic-login.service
[Unit]
Description=Disable Automatic Graphical Login

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-delay 0

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Enable and start the service
systemctl enable disable-automatic-login.service
systemctl start disable-automatic-login.service

if [ "$(id -u)" != "0" ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root."
    exit 1
fi

# Read existing sudoers configuration and check for any conflicting options
SUDOERS_CONTENT=$(sudo cat /etc/sudoers | grep '^Defaults')

# Check if the required options are already set
if [[ "$SUDOERS_CONTENT" == *$DEFAULT_SUDOERS_OPTIONS* ]]; then
    echo "The system is already configured to require the invoking user's password."
else
    # Modify sudoers file to enforce the required options
    sudo sed -i '/^Defaults/ s/^.*$/&'"$DEFAULT_SUDOERS_OPTIONS"'/' /etc/sudoers

    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "The system has been configured to require the invoking user's password for sudo."
    else
        echo "Failed to modify the sudoers file. Please check the permissions and content of the file."

    fi
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Set global lockout policy to 3 failed attempts
authselect select auth-system-auth --set-password-quality none --enable pam_pwquality.so retry=3

# Apply the changes and make them persistent across reboots
authselect apply-changes --force

# Ensure the system-auth package is installed
dnf install -y system-auth

# Update the PAM configuration to include the new lockout policy
sed -i '/pam_pwquality.so/s/^session session required pam_pwquality.so retry=3$/&/' /etc/pam.d/system-auth

# Restart the authentication service to apply the changes
systemctl restart authselect.service
```

TIME_WINDOW=900

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots by creating a systemd service unit

[Unit]
Description=Authconfig Service for Failed Login Time Window

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Enable and start the service to apply the changes immediately

sed -i '/pam_tally2.so/ { s/[^ ]*attempts=[^ ]*/attempts='$LOCKOUT_ATTEMPTS'/}' /etc/pam.d/system-auth

#!/bin/bash

# Define the character classes
upper=$(echo {A..Z})
lower=$(echo {a..z})
digits=$(echo {0..9})
special=$(echo {!@#$%^&*()-_=+[{]}|;:'",.<>/?})

# Function to check password complexity
check_password_complexity() {
    local password="$1"
    if [[ $(tr -dc '[:upper:]' <<< "$password" | wc -m) -ge 1 && \
       $(tr -dc '[:lower:]' <<< "$password" | wc -m) -ge 1 && \
       $(tr -dc '[:digit:]' <<< "$password" | wc -m) -ge 1 && \
       $(tr -dc '[:punct:]' <<< "$password" | wc -m) -ge 1 ]]; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Update the password quality policy in /etc/security/pwquality.conf

sed -i '/charclass/ s/.*/charclass = .[:upper:][:lower:][:digit:][:punct:]/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf

#!/bin/bash

# Get all local interactive users
users=$(getent passwd | grep '/home/' | cut -d: -f1)

for user in $users; do
    # Get the primary group of the user
    primary_group=$(id -gn "$user")

    # Set the umask to ensure new files are created with correct group ownership
    umask 0007

    # Loop through each file and directory in the user's home directory
    for entry in $(find /home/"$user" -type f -o -type d); do
        # Get the current group owner of the file or directory
        current_group=$(stat -c "%G" "$entry")

        # If the current group is not the primary group, change it
        if [ "$current_group" != "$primary_group" ]; then
            chown -h "$user:$primary_group" "$entry"
        fi
    done

    # Reset umask to default value
    umask 0022
done

#!/bin/bash

# Function to set correct permissions for files and directories
set_permissions() {
    local path="$1"
    if [ -d "$path" ]; then
        chmod 0750 "$path"
        find "$path" -type f ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0644 {} \;
    elif [ -f "$path" ] && ! [[ $path == .* ]]; then
        chmod 0644 "$path"
    fi
}

# Iterate over each user's home directory
while IFS= read -r user; do
    if [ "$user" != "root" ]; then
        homedir="/home/$user"
        if [ -d "$homedir" ]; then
            # Set correct permissions for the home directory itself
            set_permissions "$homedir"

            # Find all non-hidden files and directories within the home directory
            find "$homedir" -type d ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0750 {} \; -print
            find "$homedir" -type f ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0644 {} \; -print
        fi
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

# Set correct permissions for all existing files and directories in the home directories
find /home -user $(whoami) -type d ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0750 {} \;
find /home -user $(whoami) -type f ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0644 {} \;

    elif [ -f "$path" ] && ! [[ $path == .* ]]; then
        chmod 0644 "$path"
    fi
}

# Iterate over each user's home directory
while IFS= read -r user; do
    if [ "$user" != "root" ]; then
        homedir="/home/$user"
        if [ -d "$homedir" ]; then
            # Set correct permissions for the home directory itself
            set_permissions "$homedir"

            # Find all non-hidden files and directories within the home directory
            find "$homedir" -type d ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0750 {} \; -print
            find "$homedir" -type f ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0644 {} \; -print
        fi
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

# Set correct permissions for all existing files and directories in the home directories
find /home -user $(whoami) -type d ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0750 {} \;
find /home -user $(whoami) -type f ! -name '.*' -exec chmod 0644 {} \;
```

#!/bin/bash

# Check if the system is RHEL 8
if [[ "$OSTYPE" != "linux-gnu" || ! -f /etc/redhat-release ]]; then
    echo "This script should only be run on a RHEL 8 system."
    exit 1
fi

# Enable Kernel Page-Table Isolation in grub2 configuration
GRUBCONFIG="/etc/grub2/grub.cfg"
if grep -qE '^(linux|vmlinuz) [^ ]* default=0 [^ ]* earlyoom=1' "$GRUBCONFIG"; then
    echo "Kernel Page-Table Isolation is already enabled."
else
    sed -i '/^\(menuentry.*linux\)/ s/$/ \\\n\tdefault=0 \\\n\tearlyoom=1/' "$GRUBCONFIG"
fi

# Update grub2 configuration to apply changes
grub2-mkconfig -o /etc/grub2/grub.cfg

# Check if the feature is enabled for all currently installed kernels
if ! grub2-editenv list | grep -qE '^(linux|vmlinuz) [^ ]* earlyoom=1'; then
    echo "Failed to enable Kernel Page-Table Isolation."
    exit 1
fi

# Set up a systemd unit to ensure the feature persists across reboots and kernel updates
cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/kernel-pagetable-isolation.service
[Unit]
Description=Enable Kernel Page-Table Isolation

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/sbin/grub2-mkconfig -o /etc/grub2/grub.cfg

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Enable the systemd unit to run on boot
systemctl enable kernel-pagetable-isolation.service

echo "Kernel Page-Table Isolation has been successfully enabled."

#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to apply mount options to prevent device-node interpretation
apply_mount_options() {
    local mount_point=$1
    # Check if the mount point is not on the root partition
    if [[ "$mount_point" != "/" ]]; then
        # Get the current mount options for the partition
        local current_opts=$(mount | grep "^$mount_point " | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d ' ')
        # Add 'noexec,nosuid,nodev' to the existing options if not already present
        if [[ ! "$current_opts" =~ (noexec|nosuid|nodev) ]]; then
            local new_opts="${current_opts},noexec,nosuid,nodev"
            # Update the /etc/fstab entry for the partition
            sed -i "s|^$mount_point.*|$mount_point $new_opts||" /etc/fstab
        fi
    fi
}

# Get a list of all mounted partitions except the root partition
mounted_partitions=$(mount | grep '^/dev/' | awk '{print $1}' | sort)

# Apply the mount options to each non-root local partition
for partition in $mounted_partitions; do
    apply_mount_options "$partition"
done

# Re-read the /etc/fstab file to apply the changes immediately
mount -a

ExecStart+=/bin/chmod +x /etc/profile.d/capabilities.sh

#!/bin/bash

# Set up the necessary directories if they don't exist
if [ ! -d /etc/systemd/system ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system
fi

# Create a drop-in directory for custom unit files
if [ ! -d /etc/systemd/system/dropin ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/dropin
fi

# Copy the existing system.conf file to a backup location
if [ ! -f /etc/systemd/system.conf ]; then
    cp /usr/share/systemd/system.conf /etc/systemd/system.conf.bak
fi

EOF

systemctl restart systemd-manager.service

    # Update grub configuration to include the new kernel parameter

fi

#!/bin/bash

# Install SELinux policy module if not already installed
if ! rpm -q selinux-policy-module; then
    dnf install -y selinux-policy-module
fi

# Setenforce to enforcing mode
setenforce 1

# Load the custom allowlisting policy
if ! grep -q '/etc/selinux/targeted/Policy/custom_allowlist.te' /etc/selinux/targeted/Policy/teconfig; then
    cat <<EOF >> /etc/selinux/targeted/Policy/teconfig
custom_allowlist = {
    type custom_allowlist_t;
};
EOF

# Define the custom allowlisting policy
cat <<EOF > /etc/selinux/targeted/Policy/custom_allowlist.te
type custom_allowlist_t;
default_type bin_t;
range custom_allowlist_t;

# Allow binaries in custom_allowlist_t to execute themselves
allow custom_allowlist_t bin_t:execute exec_perm;

# Define the default allow rule for custom_allowlist_t
allow custom_allowlist_t self:file read;
EOF

# Apply the policy changes
semodule -i /etc/selinux/targeted/Policy/custom_allowlist.te

# Ensure the custom policy is loaded on boot
if ! grep -q 'custom_allowlist' /etc/sysconfig/selinux; then
    echo "SELINUX=enforcing" >> /etc/sysconfig/selinux
    echo "policy_module = custom_allowlist.te" >> /etc/sysconfig/selinux
fi

# Restart the SELinux policy loader to apply changes
systemctl restart libsemanage.service
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum polling interval for NTP servers
MAX_INTERVAL=64

# Check if ntp.conf exists and is writable
if [ ! -w /etc/ntp.conf ]; then
    echo "ntp.conf is not writable, please ensure it has the correct permissions."
    exit 1
fi

# Read current configuration
current_config=$(grep -v '^#' /etc/ntp.conf | grep 'server')

# Loop through each server line and set the maximum polling interval
while read -r line; do
    # Extract the server address from the line
    server=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}')
    
    # Check if the server already has a maxpoll directive
    if ! grep -q "maxpoll $MAX_INTERVAL" <<<"$current_config"; then
        # Add the maxpoll directive to the server line
        sed -i "/server $server/s/^server.*/& maxpoll $MAX_INTERVAL/" /etc/ntp.conf
    fi
done <<<"$current_config"

# Restart ntpd to apply changes
systemctl restart ntpd

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Define critical files for SSH
CRITICAL_FILES="/home/*/.ssh/authorized_keys /home/*/.ssh/config"

# Function to check file permissions and ownership
check_files() {
    for FILE in $CRITICAL_FILES; do
        if [ -e "$FILE" ]; then
            # Check if the file is world-writable
            if [ -w "$FILE" ] && ! [ -w "$FILE" -o -w "$FILE" ]; then
                echo "Critical file $FILE is world-writable. Fixing..."
                chmod o-w "$FILE"
            fi

            # Check if the owner is not root for critical files
            if [ "$(stat -c '%U' "$FILE")" != "root" ]; then
                echo "Critical file $FILE is not owned by root. Fixing..."
                chown root:root "$FILE"
            fi
        else
            # Create the file with correct permissions and ownership
            echo "Critical file $FILE does not exist. Creating..."
            touch "$FILE"
            chmod 600 "$FILE"
            chown root:root "$FILE"
        fi
    done
}

# Check and set up critical files
check_files

# Update SSH daemon configuration to enforce these checks at login
echo 'Match User *' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo '    AllowGroups *' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo '    DenyGroups *' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo '    ForceCommand /bin/false' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Reload SSH daemon to apply changes
systemctl reload sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Create an empty USBGuard policy file if it does not exist
if [ ! -f /etc/usbguard/usbguard.conf ]; then
    echo '[General]' > /etc/usbguard/usbguard.conf
    echo 'DefaultPolicy=Deny' >> /etc/usbguard/usbguard.conf
fi

# Get the list of currently connected USB devices
connected_devices=$(lsusb)

# Create a temporary file to store the device-authorization policy
temp_policy_file=$(mktemp)

# Iterate over each connected device and create an authorization rule for it
while read -r line; do
    # Extract the bus, device, and product ID from the device information
    bus=$(echo $line | awk '{print $1}' | sed 's/://')
    device=$(echo $line | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/://')
    product_id=$(echo $line | awk '{print $5}' | cut -d' ' -f2)

    # Add an authorization rule for the device to the temporary policy file
    echo "Rule=Allow" >> $temp_policy_file
    echo "Bus=$bus Device=$device ProductID=$product_id" >> $temp_policy_file
done <<< "$connected_devices"

# Replace the existing USBGuard policy with the new one
mv -f $temp_policy_file /etc/usbguard/usbguard.conf

# Restart USBGuard to apply the new policy
systemctl restart usbguard.service

# Load audit configuration if it does not exist
if [ ! -f /etc/audit/audit.rules ]; then

fi

# Add the specific rule for privileged users with login UID of 1000 or greater
auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F euid>=1000 -F auid>=1000 -F key=privileged -F perm=x -k user_account_modification
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define low disk space threshold (in percentage)
LOW_SPACE_THRESHOLD=10

# Define the path to the audit configuration file
AUDIT_CONFIG="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"

# Check if the audit daemon is running
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "Audit daemon is not running. Starting it now..."
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Ensure the audit daemon is enabled on boot
systemctl enable auditd

# Set up low disk space alert using audit rules
cat <<EOF >> /etc/audit/rules.d/low_disk_space.rules
-w / -p war -k low-disk-space
EOF

# Apply the new audit rules
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/low_disk_space.rules

# Create a script to send email alerts
cat <<EOF > /usr/local/bin/send_low_disk_space_alert.sh
#!/bin/bash
# Send an email alert when disk space is low
if [ \$1 -lt \$2 ]; then
    echo "Low disk space warning: Available disk space is below $2% on $(hostname)" | mail -s "Disk Space Alert" admin@example.com
fi
EOF

# Make the script executable
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/send_low_disk_space_alert.sh

# Add an audit rule to call the email alert script when disk space falls below threshold
cat <<EOF >> /etc/audit/rules.d/low_disk_space.rules
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S write -k low-disk-space
-w /usr/local/bin/send_low_disk_space_alert.sh -p x32 -f w
EOF

# Apply the new audit rules again
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/low_disk_space.rules

# Ensure the audit daemon is restarted to apply changes
systemctl restart auditd

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit log directory and threshold percentage
AUDIT_LOG_DIR="/var/log/audit"
THRESHOLD=25

# Calculate the disk space threshold in bytes
THRESHOLD_BYTES=$(( $(df -k "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $4}') * $((100 / THRESHOLD))))

# Check current free disk space and compare with threshold
CURRENT_FREE_SPACE=$(df -k "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $4}')
if [ "$CURRENT_FREE_SPACE" -lt "$THRESHOLD_BYTES" ]; then
    # If below threshold, set the action to notify when disk space is low
    auditctl -w "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" -p war -k disk_space_low
else
    # If above threshold, remove the action if it exists
    auditctl -D -w "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" -k disk_space_low
fi
